learning_outcomes.description: Recognise the interdisciplinary nature of modern science, including some of the similarities and differences across a range of discipline areas.
Explain the importance of modelling in science by demonstrating the skills required to produce and analyse such models.
Apply fundamental mathematical techniques that are important to problems across a range of scientific discipline areas.
Explain key introductory concepts in computer science, design and write simple computer programs in the language Python, and interpret the output of these programs.
Describe and discuss some philosophical accounts of scientific reasoning.
Communicate responses to quantitative and science-based problems in a correct, logical and scientifically appropriate style.

Develop an awareness of the importance of Australian Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander and global Indigenous Peoples’ values, cultures and knowledges and how this relates to being a contemporary scientist.

assessments.due_date:
Week 1 - Week 13

Due each week at the time of your class.
assessments.task_description:
You must attend your applied classes in-person. For each class, you will be provided with a task sheet containing relevant tasks, many of which are in the style of SCIE1000 exam questions. Some tasks will involve writing Python code. You will be encouraged to engage in discussion with your fellow classmates during classes.

There are 12 applied classes scheduled throughout the semester.

If you are unwell then DO NOT attend your in-person class. Refer to Blackboard for instructions on how to attend a different class that week if you are able.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description: This exam will assess the degree to which students have reached the learning objectives in the philosophy component of SCIE1000, by requiring them to respond to prompts discussing aspects of the scientific method.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

assessments.task_description: This assignment will assess the degree to which students have reached the learning objectives in the modelling, Python and scientific communication components of SCIE1000. Students will develop and demonstrate their skills in writing a Python program, and also demonstrate their ability to communicate science in a way which is appropriate for a prescribed target audience. The assignment will be given within a specific scientifc context, and students will use scientific information and mathematical models within their Python program.
assessments.submission_guidelines: All assignments must be submitted electronically through Blackboard by the due date and time. Plagiarism detection software will be used to check the originality of your code and other written material.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
The end of semester exam is cumulative, covering the entire course, and will focus on conceptual questions and problem-solving. It will not require you to write computer programs, but may require you to read, modify and interpret computer programs. There will be a formula sheet provided (this is available on Blackboard, under Learning Resources). There will be a reference page provided, explaining what you need to know about the scientific context of the questions - see past final examinations as examples (available on Blackboard, under Learning Resources).

The end of semester examination in this course will be held during the end-of-semester examination period. It will be an in-person, invigilated exam held on campus.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

learning_activities.topic:
Weekly lectures

One 2-hour lecture each week which will be spent working through the workbook, answering questions and discussing content.

During lectures we will discuss issues, challenge your answers, and solve problems together. During discussion times you will be asked to engage and give input into the discussions in various ways. A recording of these lectures will be available on Blackboard shortly after each class. However, we encourage you to attend live (either on campus or online) to obtain the best experience from the interactive nature of the classes. Lectures commence in Week 1.

learning_activities.topic:
Weekly applied classes

Held as two hour sessions covering problem-solving, communication in science and Python programming.

You must attend one class each week. Each class runs for two hours, in a computer laboratory (including BYOD labs), and you must attend both hours. You will enrol in an applied class at the start of the semester and should attend the same class each week if possible (in the case of illness or classes falling on public holidays, alternative arrangements will be made available). The applied classes are a great place to ask questions, get help and engage in peer-to-peer learning. One applied class will be dedicated to the philosophy of science.  Applied classes commence in Week 1.

Each class runs for two hours, in a computer laboratory (including BYOD labs), and you must attend both hours. You will enrol in an applied class at the start of the semester and should attend the same class each week if possible (in the case of illness or classes falling on public holidays, alternative arrangements will be made available). The applied classes are a great place to ask questions, get help and engage in peer-to-peer learning. One applied class will be dedicated to the philosophy of science. Applied classes commence in Week 1.

learning_activities.topic:
Additional Python lecture

There will be 1-hour lectures to cover the Python programming concepts you will be expected to learn in this course. A recording of these classes will be made available on Blackboard shortly after the session. These classes are highly recommended if you are new to computer programming. We recommend attending this class prior to attending your applied class each week. A schedule will be provided on the course Blackboard page. Python lectures commence in Week 1.
